abstract: Ten multifloral honey samples produced by *Apis mellifera* L. in Roraima, Brazil, were analyzed to determine their physicochemical properties and antioxidant potential. The study focused on quantifying total phenolic content, flavonoid profiles—specifically flavones/flavonols and flavonones/dihydroflavonols—and color intensity, as well as evaluating free-radical scavenging activity. Total phenolics, measured via the Folin-Ciocalteu method, ranged from 250 to 548 mg gallic acid kg⁻¹. Flavonoid concentrations were assessed using two distinct colorimetric assays: the aluminum chloride method yielded values between 9 and 48.6 mg quercetin kg⁻¹, while the 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine method revealed substantially higher levels of flavonones and dihydroflavonols, ranging from 1805 to 2606 mg pinocembrin kg⁻¹. Antioxidant capacity, determined by the DPPH assay, showed IC₅₀ values between 3.17 and 8.79 mg/mL, indicating high potency relative to literature standards. Correlation analysis revealed significant relationships between darker honey color, higher phenolic and flavone/flavonol concentrations, and stronger antioxidant activity. While flavonones and dihydroflavonols were major constituents, they did not significantly correlate with the other tested parameters. These findings provide the first characterization of honey from this region and underscore its potential as a source of dietary antioxidants.
